channel.title: Replica-Set on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Replica-Set on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Linux MongoDB Replica Set Not Primary
item.description:
Linux: MongoDB — replica set not primary

The not primary or not master error in MongoDB means the client attempted a write operation (insert, update, delete) against a secondary node or a node that has not been elected primary in the replica set. Only the primary node accepts writes in a standard MongoDB replica set.

What This Error Means

MongoDB replica sets use the Raft consensus protocol to elect a primary node. All writes must go to the primary, which then replicates to secondaries. When a client connects to a secondary for writes, or during an election when no primary exists, the server returns notPrimary or not master. This is by design — data consistency requires writes to go through the primary.

item.title: [Solution] mongo-go: Server Selection Timeout Fix
item.description:
mongo-go: Server Selection Timeout

This error occurs when the mongo-go driver cannot select a server from the topology within the configured timeout. It is the MongoDB driver’s equivalent of a connection timeout.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • server selection timeout: topology closed
  • server selection timeout: context deadline exceeded
  • server selection timeout: dial tcp 127.0.0.1:27017: connect: connection refused
  • no eligible servers

The mongo-go driver maintains a topology view of the MongoDB deployment. When it cannot find a suitable server for the requested operation within the timeout, it returns this error.

item.title: [Solution] mongodb Write Concern Error Fix
item.description:
mongodb Write Concern Error

Fix mongodb write concern errors. Handle write concern configuration, journaling, and majority writes.

What This Error Means

mongodb write concern errors occur when the server cannot satisfy the requested write concern:

WriteConcernError { code: 100, message: "could not find host matching read preference" }
WriteError { code: 11000, message: "duplicate key error" }

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Write concern requires majority but not enough replicas
let wc = WriteConcern::builder()
 .w(WriteConcernMode::Majority)
 .journal(true)
 .build();

// Cause 2: Primary stepped down during write
// Cause 3: Network partition prevents majority acknowledgment
// Cause 4: Journaling disabled on server
// Cause 5: wTimeout too short for slow operations

How to Fix

Fix 1: Configure appropriate write concern

use mongodb::options::{WriteConcern, WriteConcernMode};

let wc = WriteConcern::builder()
 .w(WriteConcernMode::Tags(vec![
 doc! { "dc": "east", "rack": "1" },
 ]))
 .w_timeout(std::time::Duration::from_secs(5000))
 .journal(true)
 .build();

Fix 2: Use acknowledged write concern with fallback

use mongodb::options::{WriteConcern, WriteConcernMode};

fn get_write_concern() -> WriteConcern {
 WriteConcern::builder()
 .w(WriteConcernMode::Acknowledged(1))
 .journal(false) // Don't require journaling
 .w_timeout(std::time::Duration::from_secs(10000))
 .build()
}

Fix 3: Handle write errors in bulk operations

use mongodb::bson::doc;

async fn safe_insert(collection: &Collection<Document>) -> Result<(), mongodb::error::Error> {
 let result = collection.insert_one(
 doc! { "name": "test" },
 None,
 ).await?;

 match result.inserted_id {
 mongodb::bson::Bson::ObjectId(id) => println!("Inserted: {}", id),
 _ => println!("Inserted with generated ID"),
 }

 Ok(())
}

Examples

use mongodb::{Client, options::{ClientOptions, WriteConcern}};
use mongodb::bson::doc;

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
 let client_options = ClientOptions::parse("mongodb://localhost:27017").await?;

 let client = Client::with_options(client_options)?;
 let db = client.database("mydb");
 let collection = db.collection("users");

 let result = collection.insert_one(
 doc! { "name": "Alice", "age": 30 },
 None,
 ).await?;

 println!("Inserted ID: {}", result.inserted_id);
 Ok(())
}

Related Errors

  • MongoDB Error — mongodb error
  • Connection Refused — connection refused
  • Postgres Error — postgres error

item.title: MongoDB - cannot target write to replica set
item.description:
A “cannot target write to replica set” error occurs in sharded MongoDB clusters when a write operation cannot be routed to the correct shard. This typically happens when the shard key is not included in the write operation or the cluster metadata is inconsistent.

Common Causes

  • Shard key not included in the update or delete operation
  • Shard key value cannot be determined from the operation
  • Chunk migration in progress during the write
  • Config servers are inconsistent or unreachable
  • Collection not sharded but operation expects sharded routing

How to Fix

  1. Include the shard key in all write operations:
// Bad: missing shard key
await db.collection('orders').updateOne(
 { orderId: '12345' },
 { $set: { status: 'shipped' } }
);

// Good: include shard key
await db.collection('orders').updateOne(
 { orderId: '12345', userId: 'user123' }, // userId is shard key
 { $set: { status: 'shipped' } }
);
  1. Use targeted operations with the shard key:
// Delete with shard key
await db.collection('orders').deleteOne({
 orderId: '12345',
 userId: 'user123', // shard key
});
  1. Check sharding status:
const shardInfo = await db.admin().command({ listShards: 1 });
console.log('Shards:', shardInfo.shards);

const chunks = await configServer.chunks.find({ ns: 'mydb.orders' }).toArray();
console.log('Chunks:', chunks.length);
  1. Use $merge with shard key for aggregation writes:
await db.collection('events').aggregate([
 { $group: { _id: '$userId', count: { $sum: 1 } } },
 {
 $merge: {
 into: 'userStats',
 on: '_id', // matches shard key
 }
 }
]);
  1. Resolve config server issues:
mongosh --configReplSet
rs.status()

Examples

// Error: cannot target write to replica set while sharding
await db.collection('logs').deleteMany({ timestamp: { $lt: oldDate } });
// Missing shard key in delete

// Fix: include shard key or use targeted delete
await db.collection('logs').deleteMany({
 tenantId: 'tenant1', // shard key
 timestamp: { $lt: oldDate },
});

Related Errors

  • Shard error
  • Write error

item.title: MongoDB - not primary - read preference error
item.description:
A “not primary” read preference error occurs when a read operation is sent to a secondary replica set member that is not configured to accept reads, or when the primary node is unavailable and read preference is not set for secondaries.

Common Causes

  • Default read preference sends reads to primary only
  • Primary node is down and no secondary accepts reads
  • Secondary node not configured with readPreference: secondary
  • Operations that require primary (e.g., writes) sent to secondary
  • readConcern level incompatible with read preference

How to Fix

  1. Set read preference for secondary reads:
const collection = db.collection('analytics', {
 readPreference: ReadPreference.SECONDARY_PREFERRED,
});
const results = await collection.find({}).toArray();
  1. Use read preference tags for data center routing:
const secondaryInDC = new ReadPreference('secondary', [
 { region: 'us-east', dc: 'secondary' },
]);
const collection = db.collection('logs', {
 readPreference: secondaryInDC,
});
  1. Handle primary not available:
try {
 const result = await db.collection('users').findOne({ _id: userId });
} catch (error) {
 if (error.code === 10107 || error.message.includes('not primary')) {
 // Fallback to secondary read
 const collection = db.collection('users', {
 readPreference: ReadPreference.SECONDARY_PREFERRED,
 });
 return await collection.findOne({ _id: userId });
 }
 throw error;
}
  1. Configure read concern with secondaries:
const result = await db.collection('events')
 .find({})
 .readConcern('majority')
 .toArray();
  1. Check replica set status:
const status = await db.admin().replSetGetStatus();
console.log('Primary:', status.members.find(m => m.stateStr === 'PRIMARY')?.name);

Examples

// Error: not primary and secondaryOk=false
const result = await db.collection('users').findOne({ _id: 1 });
// When connected to secondary without read preference

// Fix: set read preference
const collection = db.collection('users', {
 readPreference: ReadPreference.SECONDARY_PREFERRED,
});
const result = await collection.findOne({ _id: 1 });

Related Errors

  • Replica set error
  • Connection error

item.title: MongoDB OplogWindowTooSmall: oplog window exhausted
item.description:
This error occurs when a secondary member in a replica set cannot keep up with the primary and falls out of the oplog window. The secondary must do a full initial sync to rejoin.

Common Causes

  • Write volume exceeds the secondary’s replication throughput
  • Oplog size is too small for the amount of write activity
  • Secondary hardware is underpowered (CPU, disk I/O)
  • Network latency between primary and secondary

How to Fix

  1. Check current oplog window size:
rs.printReplicationInfo() // primary
rs.printSecondaryReplicationInfo() // secondaries
  1. Resize the oplog on the primary:
use local
db.runCommand({
 replSetResizeOplog: 1,
 size: 16 * 1024 * 1024 * 1024 // 16 GB
})
  1. Monitor replication lag:
rs.status().members.forEach(m => {
 print(`${m.name} lag: ${m.optimeDate - rs.status().date} ms`)
})
  1. Upgrade secondary hardware or add more secondaries to distribute load.

Examples

rs.printSecondaryReplicationInfo()
// source: secondary1:27017
// syncedTo: Thu Jul 16 2026 10:00:00 GMT+0000
// 0 secs (0 hrs) behind the primary
// source: secondary2:27017
// syncedTo: Thu Jul 16 2026 09:00:00 GMT+0000
// 3600 secs (1 hrs) behind the primary — likely will fall out of window

Related Errors

  • MongoDB WriteConcernError

item.title: MongoDB Replica Set Error
item.description:
MongoDB Replica Set Error

A MongoDB replica set error occurs when the replica set encounters issues with member elections, data synchronization, or member health. This affects high availability and data redundancy.

Common Causes

  • Primary node unavailable or crashed
  • Network partition between members
  • Insufficient replica set members for quorum
  • Member lagging too far behind in replication

How to Fix

Check Replica Set Status

rs.status()
rs.printReplicationInfo()
rs.printSecondaryReplicationInfo()

Check Member Health

rs.status().members.forEach(m => {
 print(m.name, m.stateStr, m.optimeDate)
})

Force Election

rs.stepDown()
// Or on a secondary
rs.syncFrom('primary:27017')

Add Missing Member

rs.add('newmember:27017')

Fix Network Connectivity

# Ensure all members can communicate
mongo --host member1:27017 --eval "rs.status()"

Configure Replica Set

rs.initiate({
 _id: 'rs0',
 members: [
 { _id: 0, host: 'member1:27017' },
 { _id: 1, host: 'member2:27017' },
 { _id: 2, host: 'member3:27017' }
 ]
})

Examples

// No primary
rs.status().myState // 2 (secondary)
MongoError: no primary found in replica set

// Fix: check member health and force election
rs.status().members

Related Errors

  • Connection Error — connection failure
  • Write Error — write concern error

item.title: MongoDB WriteConcernError: not enough data-bearing nodes
item.description:
This error occurs when a write operation specifies a write concern (e.g. w: 3 or w: "majority") but not enough data-bearing replica set members are available to satisfy it.

Common Causes

  • A replica set member has gone down or is lagging behind
  • The write concern level is set higher than the number of healthy data-bearing nodes
  • Network partition between the application and replica set members
  • Misconfigured write concern in connection options

How to Fix

  1. Check replica set status:
rs.status()
  1. Use a more lenient write concern for non-critical writes:
db.collection.insertOne({ name: "Alice" }, { writeConcern: { w: 1 } })
  1. Adjust the connection string write concern:
const client = new MongoClient(uri, {
 writeConcern: { w: "majority", wtimeoutMS: 5000 }
})
  1. Add wtimeout to avoid indefinite blocking:
db.collection.insertOne(
 { name: "Alice" },
 { writeConcern: { w: "majority", wtimeout: 5000 } }
)

Examples

// Replicaset with 2 members, write concern w: 3
db.users.insertOne({ name: "Alice" }, { writeConcern: { w: 3 } })
// WriteConcernError: not enough data-bearing nodes

Related Errors

  • E11000 duplicate key error collection
